This means 60% of the gain or loss is treated as long-term capital gain or loss, and 40% is treated as short-term capital gain or loss.

questions.question: Vista Healthcare LLC sold equipment used in its medical practice. The original cost was $50,000, accumulated depreciation was $20,000, and the selling price was $40,000. What is the tax treatment of the Section 1245 depreciation recapture?
options: Passive income, subject to limitations based on the taxpayer's AGI and participation level.
Capital gain, taxed at a preferential rate if the equipment was held for more than one year.
Ordinary income up to the amount of depreciation previously allowed
Tax-free income because the selling price did not exceed the original cost of the equipment.
questions.explanation: Section 1245 recaptures depreciation on personal property as ordinary income to the extent of gain, up to depreciation previously allowed or allowable.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Section 1245 recapture is always treated as ordinary income, not passive income. Passive income rules apply to activities where the taxpayer doesn't materially participate.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the *amount exceeding* the original cost less accumulated depreciation may be a capital gain, the recapture portion is always ordinary income. Section 1245 specifically overrides capital gain treatment for depreciation recapture.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Section 1245 requires that any gain on the sale of depreciable personal property (or certain other property) be treated as ordinary income to the extent of depreciation previously allowed or allowable. This prevents taxpayers from converting ordinary income (depreciation deductions) into capital gains.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The selling price exceeding the adjusted basis triggers depreciation recapture, regardless of whether it exceeds the original cost. The original cost is irrelevant for determining depreciation recapture.

questions.educational: Section 1245 exists to prevent taxpayers from taking ordinary deductions (depreciation) and then later selling the asset and recognizing a capital gain. This 'recapture' rule ensures that the portion of the gain attributable to prior depreciation is taxed at ordinary income rates, reflecting the tax benefit previously received. Understanding depreciation recapture is crucial for accurately calculating taxable income and advising clients on the tax implications of asset sales.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that Section 1245 primarily applies to personal property and aims to recharacterize gains up to the amount of depreciation taken, while Section 1250 applies to real property and may have different recapture rules depending on the type of depreciation used.
questions.memoryAid: 1245: 'Personal Property, Ordinary Income to the extent of Depreciation'
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse Section 1245 recapture with capital gains. Section 1245 specifically treats the gain up to the amount of depreciation previously allowed as ordinary income, not capital gain. The remaining gain, if any, may be Section 1231 gain, which could be capital gain.

questions.question: Robert sold a rental property for a gain. A portion of the gain is attributable to depreciation taken after 1986. At what maximum rate is the unrecaptured Section 1250 gain taxed?
questions.explanation: Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain (straight-line depreciation on real property) is taxed at a maximum rate of 25%, not the 0%/15%/20% capital gains rates.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A 20% rate applies to other types of capital gains, such as gains from the sale of stocks held for more than one year, but not to unrecaptured Section 1250 gain.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The 28% rate applies to collectibles gains and small business stock gains, not to unrecaptured Section 1250 gain.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain, which is the amount of depreciation taken on real property that is not subject to recapture as ordinary income, is taxed at a maximum rate of 25%. This preferential rate acknowledges the nature of real estate depreciation and its impact on investment decisions.
whyWrong.3: Why option C is WRONG - While 15% (or 0%) is a common capital gains rate, it does not apply to unrecaptured Section 1250 gain. Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain has its own specific maximum tax rate.

questions.educational: The unrecaptured Section 1250 gain rules exist because Congress wanted to encourage investment in real estate while also ensuring that some of the tax benefits from depreciation are eventually recaptured. This gain represents the accumulated depreciation taken on real property and is taxed at a rate higher than the standard capital gains rate but lower than ordinary income rates, striking a balance between incentivizing investment and recouping tax benefits.
questions.examTip: Remember that unrecaptured Section 1250 gain is a common area tested on the EA exam, so be sure to memorize the 25% maximum tax rate and understand when it applies, especially when dealing with rental properties.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '1250' as '1 quarter' or 25 cents on the dollar – 25%.
questions.commonMistake: A common trap is to confuse the tax rates applicable to different types of capital gains. Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain, which is depreciation taken on real property after 1986, is taxed at a maximum rate of 25%. Other capital gains rates, such as 20% and 28%, apply to different types of assets.

questions.question:
Green Acres Realty is considering selling a commercial building and acquiring another. After the TCJA, like-kind (Section 1031) exchanges are available for:
A) Personal property only | B) Securities and real property | C) Real property only | D) All business property
options: Personal property only
Securities and real property
Real property only
All business property
questions.explanation: TCJA limited Section 1031 to real property only. Personal property like-kind exchanges are no longer permitted for exchanges after December 31, 2017.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) eliminated like-kind exchanges for personal property. Therefore, personal property is not eligible for Section 1031 treatment after December 31, 2017.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While real property is eligible for like-kind exchanges under Section 1031 after TCJA, securities are explicitly excluded. Securities are generally considered 'property held primarily for sale' and are ineligible for Section 1031 treatment.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) limited Section 1031 like-kind exchanges to real property only, effective for exchanges completed after December 31, 2017. This means that only real property held for productive use in a trade or business or for investment can be exchanged for like-kind real property.
whyWrong.3: Why option C is WRONG - The TCJA significantly narrowed the scope of Section 1031 exchanges. While some business property might qualify if it is real property, the law specifically restricts like-kind exchanges to real property only, excluding other types of business property like equipment or inventory.

questions.educational: Section 1031 allows taxpayers to defer capital gains taxes when exchanging property held for productive use in a trade or business or for investment for like-kind property. The TCJA's limitation to real property reflects a policy shift towards generating more immediate tax revenue by eliminating deferral opportunities for personal property. Understanding the nuances of Section 1031 is crucial for the EA exam, as it directly impacts tax planning strategies for clients with real estate holdings.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates provided in the fact pattern of Section 1031 questions. If the exchange occurred after December 31, 2017, remember that it must involve real property to qualify.
questions.memoryAid: TCJA = The Cut Just Applies to Real (property).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly believe that like-kind exchanges are available for all types of property. After the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA), like-kind exchanges are generally limited to real property only. The exam exploits the misconception that personal property or securities still qualify for Section 1031 treatment.

questions.question:
Davis Investments is selling an apartment building and plans to acquire a replacement property through a Section 1031 exchange. To qualify for full deferral, the replacement property must be identified within:
A) 90 days | B) 60 days | C) 45 days | D) 30 days
options: 90 days
60 days
45 days
30 days
questions.explanation: Replacement property must be identified within 45 days of the sale of relinquished property. Exchange must be completed within 180 days or tax return due date.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The 90-day period is not related to the identification or completion deadlines for a Section 1031 exchange. The 45-day and 180-day rules are strictly enforced by the IRS.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A 60-day period is not a deadline related to Section 1031 exchanges. The identification period is shorter than this, and the exchange period is longer.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - In a Section 1031 exchange, the replacement property must be identified within 45 days from the date the taxpayer transfers the relinquished property. This identification period is crucial for meeting the requirements of Section 1031 and deferring capital gains tax.
whyWrong.3: Why option C is WRONG - A 30-day period is not the correct timeframe for identifying replacement property in a Section 1031 exchange. The identification period is 45 days, and using 30 days would jeopardize the tax-deferred status of the exchange.

questions.educational: Section 1031 allows for the deferral of capital gains tax when exchanging like-kind property used in a trade or business or held for investment. The 45-day identification period and the 180-day exchange period are strict deadlines. Failure to meet these deadlines will disqualify the exchange and trigger immediate taxation of any realized gain. As an EA, understanding these rules is critical for advising clients on tax-efficient real estate transactions.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 45-day identification period includes weekends and holidays. Advise your clients to start the identification process immediately after transferring the relinquished property to avoid missing the deadline.
questions.memoryAid: 45 to IDENTIFY, 180 to BUY (complete the exchange).
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly recall the time limits for identifying and acquiring replacement property in a 1031 exchange. The 45-day identification period is frequently confused with the 180-day acquisition period. The exam exploits this memory lapse.

questions.question:
Maria exchanged a rental property for another, but also received $20,000 cash and a boat worth $10,000. In a 1031 exchange, "boot" refers to:
A) Deferred gain | B) The relinquished property | C) Non-like-kind property or cash received, taxable to extent of realized gain | D) The replacement property
options: Gain that is automatically deferred indefinitely, regardless of boot received.
The original property given up in the exchange, whose basis carries over.
Non-like-kind property or cash received, taxable to extent of realized gain
The new property acquired in the exchange, which is always tax-free in a 1031.
questions.explanation: Boot is non-like-kind property (cash, personal property, debt relief) received in an exchange. Boot is taxable to the extent of realized gain.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Boot triggers taxable gain to the extent of the boot received, so gain is not automatically deferred. The presence of boot changes the tax implications.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the basis of the relinquished property does carry over (with adjustments), 'boot' refers specifically to the non-like-kind property received, not the property given up.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - 'Boot' in a 1031 exchange refers to any non-like-kind property or cash received by the taxpayer. This 'boot' triggers taxable gain to the extent of the realized gain on the exchange, but not exceeding the value of the boot received. Section 1031(b) specifically addresses the treatment of boot in an otherwise qualifying exchange.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The replacement property itself isn't the 'boot.' While the receipt of the replacement property is generally tax-free, the presence of boot can trigger taxable gain, even with the new property.

questions.educational: Section 1031 allows for the deferral of capital gains taxes when exchanging like-kind property held for productive use in a trade or business or for investment. The concept of 'boot' is crucial because it represents a deviation from the purely like-kind exchange, triggering taxable gain. Understanding boot is essential for advising clients on the tax implications of real estate transactions and ensuring compliance with IRS regulations.
questions.examTip: Remember that debt relief is considered 'boot' received in a 1031 exchange. If the taxpayer's debt on the relinquished property is greater than the debt on the replacement property, the difference is treated as cash received and can trigger taxable gain.
questions.memoryAid: BOOT: Bothering Other Opportunities with Taxes (because it triggers taxable gain).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates misunderstand the concept of 'boot' in a 1031 exchange. They may think it refers to the entire gain or the relinquished property itself. The exam tests the understanding that 'boot' is non-like-kind property that triggers taxable gain to the extent of the realized gain.

questions.question:
John sells land to his sister, and she intends to perform a 1031 exchange. A 1031 exchange with a related party requires:
A) No special rules | B) Immediate gain recognition | C) Both parties hold property for 2 years or gain is recognized | D) IRS approval
options: Related party exchanges follow standard 1031 guidelines without modification.
The related party transfer triggers immediate gain recognition for the transferor.
Both parties hold property for 2 years or gain is recognized
The 1031 exchange requires IRS pre-approval to ensure compliance with regulations.
questions.explanation: Related party 1031 exchanges require both parties to hold their property for 2 years. If either disposes early, deferred gain is recognized.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Related party exchanges have specific holding period requirements that deviate from standard 1031 rules.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Immediate gain recognition is not automatic; it depends on whether the holding period requirements are met.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - IRC Section 1031(f) stipulates that if a related party disposes of the property received in a 1031 exchange within two years, the original exchange is disqualified, and gain is recognized as of the date of the disposition. This rule prevents taxpayers from using related parties to quickly cash out of an investment while deferring taxes.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 1031 exchanges do not require IRS pre-approval; they are self-assessed and reported on tax returns.

questions.educational: The related party rules in 1031 exchanges are designed to prevent taxpayers from using the like-kind exchange provisions to essentially sell property while deferring capital gains taxes. Without these rules, a taxpayer could exchange property with a related party and then have the related party immediately sell the property, effectively circumventing the tax implications of a direct sale. Understanding these nuances is crucial for advising clients on tax-efficient real estate transactions.
questions.examTip: Remember that the two-year holding period for related party 1031 exchanges applies to BOTH parties involved. If either party disposes of the property early, the gain is triggered for the original exchanger.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Two to Tango': Both related parties must hold the property for TWO years, or the tax deferral is GONE.
questions.commonMistake: A common trap is overlooking the related-party rules in 1031 exchanges. Candidates may assume that as long as a 1031 exchange is properly structured, it's automatically valid, even with related parties. The exam tests the knowledge that both parties must hold the property for at least two years to avoid gain recognition.

questions.question: Under involuntary conversion (Section 1033), gain is deferred if replacement property is acquired within:
options: 1 year from the close of the tax year in which the conversion occurred.
5 years, but only if the conversion was due to a natural disaster.
2 years (3 years for real property condemned)
180 days, but only if the conversion involves personal property.
questions.explanation: Section 1033 requires replacement within 2 years (3 years for condemned real property) after the close of the first tax year in which any gain is realized.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The general replacement period is 2 years, not 1 year, from the close of the tax year in which the conversion occurred (3 years for condemned real property).
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The 5-year replacement period applies to conversions due to Presidentially declared disasters, but only for the purchase of replacement property. The general rule is 2 years (3 years for condemned real property).
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Section 1033 allows for a deferral of gain if the taxpayer acquires qualifying replacement property within two years from the close of the tax year in which the gain was realized. This period is extended to three years for real property that is condemned (e.g., taken by eminent domain).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The 180-day replacement period applies to the rollover of capital gains from qualified opportunity funds, not involuntary conversions. The general rule is 2 years (3 years for condemned real property).

questions.educational: Section 1033 allows taxpayers to defer recognizing a gain when property is involuntarily converted (destroyed, stolen, condemned, etc.) and replaced with similar property. The purpose is to alleviate the tax burden on taxpayers who are forced to dispose of property and reinvest in similar assets, promoting economic stability. This deferral impacts basis calculations for the replacement property, a key concept for understanding future tax implications.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the type of involuntary conversion (e.g., condemnation of real property) as the replacement period can vary. On the EA exam, carefully read the facts to determine if the standard 2-year or extended 3-year replacement period applies.
questions.memoryAid: Think '2-3' – 2 years generally, 3 years for condemned real property.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often mix up the replacement periods for different types of involuntary conversions. The general rule is two years, but it extends to three years for real property condemned. The exam exploits this confusion by offering shorter timeframes as distractors.

questions.question:
A fire destroyed Sarah's warehouse, which had an adjusted basis of $200,000. She received $300,000 in insurance proceeds. If insurance proceeds exceed the basis of destroyed property:
A) Proceeds are always excluded | B) All proceeds are taxable | C) Gain can be deferred under Section 1033 if replacement property acquired | D) No gain recognized
options: Insurance proceeds are always excluded from gross income, regardless of the basis or replacement plans.
All insurance proceeds received due to involuntary conversion are taxable as ordinary income in the year received.
Gain can be deferred under Section 1033 if replacement property acquired
No gain is recognized on involuntary conversions, as the event was not a voluntary sale or exchange by the taxpayer.
questions.explanation: Casualty gains from insurance can be deferred under involuntary conversion rules by reinvesting in similar property. Otherwise, gain is recognized.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Insurance proceeds are not always excluded; gain may be recognized. Section 1033 allows for deferral, not outright exclusion.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Not all proceeds are taxable; Section 1033 allows for deferral of gain if replacement property is acquired. Also, the gain is not necessarily ordinary income.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Section 1033 allows for the deferral of gain recognition when property is involuntarily converted (e.g., destroyed by casualty) and the taxpayer reinvests the proceeds in similar property within a specified time period. This allows taxpayers to replace essential assets without immediate tax consequences, promoting economic recovery after a loss.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Gain *can* be recognized on involuntary conversions if the proceeds exceed the basis and are not reinvested in qualified replacement property under Section 1033.

questions.educational: Section 1033 provides an exception to the general rule that gains are taxable. This exception is designed to alleviate the burden on taxpayers who experience involuntary conversions and reinvest in similar property. The gain is deferred, not eliminated, as the basis of the replacement property is reduced by the amount of the deferred gain. This is a common area tested on the EA exam, particularly in SEE1.
questions.examTip: When calculating gain or loss from a casualty, remember to first determine the adjusted basis of the property, then compare it to the insurance proceeds received. Pay close attention to whether the taxpayer elects to defer the gain under Section 1033, as this will impact the basis of the replacement property.
questions.memoryAid: IC-Reinvest: Involuntary Conversion - Reinvest to defer gain.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates fail to recognize that insurance proceeds exceeding the basis of destroyed property can result in a gain. They may incorrectly assume that insurance proceeds are always tax-free or that no gain is recognized. The exam tests the understanding that a gain exists, but can be deferred if replacement property is acquired.

questions.question:
Mark is reviewing health savings options for his clients. Archer MSAs are:
A) The same as HSAs | B) Still available for new contributions | C) Grandfathered for existing accounts only, no new MSAs since 2007 | D) Replaced by Roth IRAs
options: Subject to the same contribution limits and eligibility requirements as HSAs, but with fewer investment options.
Still available for new contributions if the individual meets specific pre-2008 eligibility requirements and has no other health coverage.
Grandfathered for existing accounts only, no new MSAs since 2007
Replaced by Roth IRAs as a tax-advantaged savings vehicle for healthcare expenses in retirement, offering similar contribution rules.
questions.explanation: Archer MSAs were available before HSAs. No new Archer MSAs can be established, but existing accounts remain valid with similar tax treatment to HSAs.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Archer MSAs and HSAs have different eligibility requirements and contribution limits. Archer MSAs also have fewer investment options.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - New Archer MSAs have not been available since 2007. They are grandfathered for those who had them before that date.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Archer MSAs were an earlier version of health savings accounts. Due to changes in legislation, no new Archer MSAs have been allowed since 2007, but those established before that date are grandfathered and can continue to be used.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Roth IRAs are not a direct replacement for Archer MSAs. While they can be used for healthcare expenses in retirement, they are primarily retirement savings vehicles and have different contribution rules and tax implications.

questions.educational: Archer MSAs were a precursor to HSAs, designed to help self-employed individuals and small business employees save for medical expenses. The shift to HSAs aimed to broaden access to tax-advantaged health savings. Understanding the historical context of Archer MSAs helps in recognizing their limited current relevance but also reinforces the broader concept of tax-advantaged health savings options, a key area on the exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to dates and phase-out periods related to tax law changes. Knowing that Archer MSAs are no longer available for new accounts is crucial for correctly answering questions about health savings options.
questions.memoryAid: A-OK (Archer-Old-Kinda) - helps remember Archer MSAs are older and no longer actively offered.
questions.commonMistake: The exam exploits the misconception that Archer MSAs are still widely available. Many candidates are unaware that new Archer MSAs have been largely replaced by HSAs. The question tests knowledge of the historical context and current status of Archer MSAs.

questions.question:
Susan, age 58, uses funds from her HSA to pay for a vacation. HSA distributions for non-qualified expenses by someone under 65 are:
A) Tax-free | B) Subject to 50% penalty | C) Included in income plus 20% penalty | D) 10% penalty only
options: Tax-free if used within the same calendar year
Subject to 50% penalty, but only on amounts over $10,000
Included in income plus 20% penalty
Subject to a 10% penalty, but only if over age 62
questions.explanation: Non-qualified HSA distributions are included in gross income and subject to a 20% additional tax penalty if under age 65. After 65, only income tax applies.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - HSA distributions for non-qualified expenses are never tax-free for those under 65; they are always included in income and subject to a penalty.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The penalty for non-qualified HSA distributions for those under 65 is 20%, not 50%, and there is no dollar limit exception.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - HSA distributions used for non-qualified expenses by individuals under age 65 are included in gross income and are subject to an additional 20% penalty. This is designed to incentivize the use of HSAs for healthcare expenses.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The penalty for non-qualified HSA distributions is 20% for those under 65, not 10%, and age 62 is irrelevant.

questions.educational: Health Savings Accounts (HSAs) are designed to help individuals save for healthcare expenses. To encourage this, distributions for non-qualified expenses are penalized, especially for those under 65. This penalty, along with the inclusion of the distribution in income, ensures that HSAs are primarily used for their intended purpose and prevents them from becoming general savings accounts.
questions.examTip: Remember the age thresholds for HSA distributions: under 65 incurs a 20% penalty for non-qualified expenses, while those 65 and older only pay income tax on non-qualified distributions. This is a common point of confusion on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: HSA: Health Savings Account. Under 65? Non-qualified = Income + Twenty (20%) penalty!
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often confuse the penalties associated with non-qualified HSA distributions. The penalty for those under 65 is higher than the penalty for early distributions from retirement accounts. The exam tests the specific penalty amount and the fact that the distribution is also included in income.

questions.question:
David wants to maximize his HSA contributions. He enrolls in a HDHP on December 1st. The HSA last-month rule allows full-year contribution if:
A) Any day of coverage in the year | B) Only if started January 1 | C) HDHP coverage on December 1, but must maintain 13 months or recapture applies | D) HDHP coverage all 12 months
options: Any day of HDHP coverage in December allows full HSA contribution, regardless of prior coverage.
Only if HDHP coverage began on January 1st, with continuous coverage throughout the entire calendar year.
HDHP coverage on December 1, but must maintain 13 months or recapture applies
HDHP coverage for all 12 months, regardless of enrollment date, allows for full HSA contribution without penalty.
questions.explanation: If eligible on December 1, the full annual contribution is allowed. However, coverage must be maintained for entire following year (testing period) or excess is taxable plus 10% penalty.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The last-month rule requires HDHP coverage on December 1st, not just any day in December. It also requires maintaining coverage for 13 months.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The last-month rule allows for full contribution even if HDHP coverage starts later than January 1st, provided the December 1st and 13-month rule are met.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - The last-month rule allows an individual covered by an HDHP on December 1st to contribute the maximum HSA amount for the year, as if they were covered for the entire year. However, to avoid penalties, they must remain covered by an HDHP for the entire following year (the 'testing period'). If coverage lapses, the contributions are subject to income tax and a 10% penalty, except in cases of death or disability.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The last-month rule allows for full contribution even with less than 12 months of coverage, provided the December 1st and 13-month rule are met.

questions.educational: The HSA last-month rule provides flexibility for individuals who become eligible for an HDHP late in the tax year. Without this rule, they would be limited to contributing only a fraction of the maximum HSA contribution. This rule is tied to the concept of encouraging individuals to utilize high-deductible health plans and save for future medical expenses, a key component of healthcare tax policy.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates of HDHP coverage and whether the individual maintained coverage throughout the testing period. Remember to calculate the recapture amount if the testing period requirement is not met, including both the income tax and the 10% penalty.
questions.memoryAid: December 1st, Stay Covered, or 'D1SC' - helps remember the December 1st requirement and the need to 'Stay Covered' for the testing period.
questions.commonMistake: A common trap is misunderstanding the HSA last-month rule and the testing period. Candidates may incorrectly believe that any coverage in December is sufficient or that coverage for the entire year is required. The exam tests the specific requirements of maintaining coverage for 13 months to avoid recapture.

questions.question: John rolled over funds from a traditional IRA at Vanguard to another IRA at Fidelity on March 15, 2023. He is now considering another rollover in January 2024. The IRA one-rollover-per-year rule limits:
options: Annual IRA contributions, limiting them to $6,500, plus catch-up if eligible
Roth conversions, but only if the taxpayer's AGI exceeds certain limits
60-day rollovers to one per 12-month period across all IRAs
Trustee-to-trustee transfers, but only if they involve employer-sponsored plans
questions.explanation: Only one 60-day rollover is permitted in any 12-month period for all IRAs combined. This does not apply to trustee-to-trustee transfers or Roth conversions.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The one-rollover-per-year rule doesn't limit contributions; contribution limits are separate and based on age.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The one-rollover-per-year rule applies regardless of AGI and is unrelated to Roth conversion eligibility.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - The one-rollover-per-year rule, as defined by the IRS, states that only one 60-day rollover is permitted in any 12-month period across all of an individual's IRAs. This rule aims to prevent individuals from using rollovers as a means of short-term borrowing from their retirement accounts.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The one-rollover-per-year rule applies to all IRA rollovers, not just those involving employer-sponsored plans. Trustee-to-trustee transfers are not limited by the one-rollover-per-year rule.

questions.educational: The one-rollover-per-year rule is designed to prevent abuse of the rollover process, specifically avoiding the use of IRA funds as a short-term loan. Understanding this rule is crucial for advising clients on proper retirement planning and avoiding potential tax penalties. This rule falls under the broader topic of retirement distributions and rollovers, a key area tested on the EA exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the one-rollover-per-year rule applies across *all* of an individual's IRAs combined, not per individual IRA account.
questions.memoryAid: Think '1-12-All': One rollover per 12-month period across All IRAs.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the one-rollover-per-year rule with other IRA limitations. They may think it applies to contributions or Roth conversions. The exam tests the understanding that it specifically limits 60-day rollovers between IRAs to one per 12-month period, regardless of the number of IRAs owned.

questions.question: Sarah is a stay-at-home mother with no earned income. Her husband, David, earned $80,000 this year. They want to contribute to an IRA for Sarah. A spousal IRA contribution can be made for Sarah if:
options: Both spouses are over 70½ and neither is still employed, as this allows penalty-free withdrawals.
Only one spouse is under 50 and therefore has a longer time horizon for tax-deferred growth.
Filing jointly and working spouse has enough compensation to cover both contributions
They have no income at all, but are receiving Social Security benefits, which count as compensation.
questions.explanation: A non-working spouse can receive IRA contributions up to the annual limit if the couple files jointly and the working spouse has compensation covering total contributions.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Age 70½ is irrelevant for spousal IRA eligibility; it only affects Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) for traditional IRAs and Roth IRA contributions before 2020.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The age of either spouse is not a factor in determining eligibility for a spousal IRA contribution.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - A spousal IRA allows a working spouse to contribute to an IRA for their non-working spouse, up to the annual contribution limit, provided they file a joint return and the working spouse's compensation is at least equal to the total IRA contributions for both spouses. This is a key provision in retirement planning for married couples.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Having no income at all disqualifies them, as the working spouse needs compensation to cover the contribution; Social Security benefits do not count as compensation for IRA purposes.

questions.educational: The spousal IRA provision exists to allow couples where one spouse is not employed, or has significantly lower earnings, to save for retirement. It recognizes that both spouses contribute to the household, even if one is not directly earning income. This is an important aspect of retirement planning and is often tested in conjunction with other retirement savings vehicles like 401(k)s and Roth IRAs, as well as the deductibility of IRA contributions based on income and retirement plan coverage.
questions.examTip: Remember to check the filing status when answering IRA questions. Filing separately generally disqualifies a couple from making spousal IRA contributions. Also, be aware of the income limitations for deducting traditional IRA contributions if either spouse is covered by a retirement plan at work.
questions.memoryAid: S-I-J: Spousal IRA requires Sufficient income (of working spouse) and Joint filing.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume that a stay-at-home spouse cannot contribute to an IRA. The exam tests the understanding of the spousal IRA rules, which allow contributions for a non-working spouse if the working spouse has sufficient compensation and they file jointly.

questions.question: Mary passed away in July 2023, leaving her traditional IRA to her adult daughter, Emily. For deaths after 2019, most non-spouse beneficiaries of traditional IRAs must:
options: Take an immediate lump sum distribution, paying all taxes at once
Roll over the inherited IRA into their own IRA, deferring taxes
Withdraw entire balance within 10 years (SECURE Act)
Calculate Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) over their life expectancy
questions.explanation: Under SECURE Act, most non-spouse beneficiaries must empty the account within 10 years. Exceptions: eligible designated beneficiaries (spouse, minor, disabled, chronically ill, or within 10 years of age).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a lump sum distribution is *an* option, it's not the *only* option or the *required* action. The 10-year rule applies.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Non-spouse beneficiaries cannot roll over inherited IRAs into their own IRAs. This is only allowed for spouses.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - The SECURE Act of 2019 significantly changed the rules for inherited IRAs. For deaths after 2019, most non-spouse beneficiaries must withdraw the entire balance of the inherited IRA within 10 years of the original owner's death. This rule accelerates tax revenue for the government.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The life expectancy rule for inherited IRAs only applies if the original IRA owner died before their required beginning date and the beneficiary is an 'eligible designated beneficiary'. Otherwise, the 10-year rule applies.

questions.educational: The SECURE Act aimed to increase retirement savings and simplify rules, but it also accelerated tax collection on inherited retirement accounts. The 10-year rule forces beneficiaries to recognize the income within a decade, preventing the prolonged tax deferral that was possible under the old 'stretch IRA' rules. Understanding the SECURE Act's impact on retirement planning is crucial for advising clients on estate planning and tax strategies.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the date of death when dealing with inherited IRAs. If the death occurred after 2019, the 10-year rule is likely in effect unless an exception applies. Remember the 'eligible designated beneficiary' exceptions.
questions.memoryAid: SECURE Act = See Cure Act = See you later to the 'stretch IRA' and hello to the 10-year rule (for most).
questions.authorityRef: IRC §401(a)(9)(H); Treas. Reg. §1.401(a)(9)-5, Q&A-4; SECURE Act of 2019
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the rules for spousal beneficiaries with those for non-spouse beneficiaries. A common trap is assuming that non-spouse beneficiaries can always roll over inherited IRA assets into their own IRAs, which is generally not allowed. The 10-year rule introduced by the SECURE Act is also frequently overlooked.

questions.question: Robert passed away in August 2023, leaving his Roth IRA to his son, Michael. Robert established the Roth IRA in 2015. Inherited Roth IRA distributions for non-spouse beneficiaries (deaths after 2019) are:
options: Always taxable as ordinary income, regardless of the 5-year rule being met.
Subject to a 10% penalty if distributions occur before Michael reaches age 59½.
Tax-free if 5-year rule met; still subject to 10-year withdrawal requirement
Excluded from income only if Michael is over age 59½ at the time of distribution, otherwise taxable.
questions.explanation: Inherited Roth distributions are tax-free if the 5-year rule is satisfied (from original owner's first contribution). The 10-year rule still requires full distribution within 10 years.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Roth IRA distributions are tax-free if the 5-year rule is met, even for non-spouse beneficiaries. This option incorrectly states that they are always taxable.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The 10% penalty does not apply to *inherited* Roth IRAs, regardless of the beneficiary's age. This option incorrectly applies the early withdrawal penalty.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Inherited Roth IRA distributions are tax-free if the 5-year rule is satisfied, meaning five years have passed since the first contribution to the Roth IRA. However, for deaths after 2019, the SECURE Act requires non-spouse beneficiaries to withdraw the entire account within 10 years of the original owner's death.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The beneficiary's age is irrelevant for inherited Roth IRA distributions. The tax treatment depends on the 5-year rule and the 10-year rule, not the beneficiary's age.

questions.educational: The tax advantages of Roth IRAs extend to beneficiaries, but with specific rules. The 5-year rule ensures the Roth IRA has seasoned before distributions are entirely tax-free. The 10-year rule under the SECURE Act accelerates the distribution timeline for inherited retirement accounts, impacting estate planning and tax strategies.
questions.examTip: Remember the 5-year rule and the 10-year rule for inherited Roth IRAs. The 5-year rule determines taxability, while the 10-year rule dictates the distribution timeline for deaths after 2019. Be prepared to advise clients on these rules when planning their estates.
questions.memoryAid: 5-Year Rule = Tax-Free, 10-Year Rule = Distribution Timeline (post-2019 deaths)
questions.authorityRef: IRC §408A(d)(3); Treas. Reg. §1.408A-6, Q&A-7; SECURE Act of 2019
questions.commonMistake: Many test-takers incorrectly assume that all inherited Roth IRA distributions are taxable. The exam exploits the misconception that the 5-year rule doesn't apply to inherited Roth IRAs. Candidates also often forget that the 10-year rule applies to deaths after 2019, even if the 5-year rule is satisfied.

questions.question: Susan will turn 72 on November 1, 2023. She wants to understand when she needs to start taking Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs). For those turning 72 after December 31, 2022, the RMD required beginning date age is:
questions.explanation: SECURE 2.0 raised RMD age to 73 for those turning 72 after 2022. It increases to 75 for those born after 1960 (starting in 2033).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The age of 72 was the RMD age for those who turned 70 1/2 before January 1, 2023. The SECURE 2.0 Act changed the RMD age for those turning 72 after December 31, 2022.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The age of 70½ was the RMD age for many years, but it was increased to 72 by the SECURE Act of 2019 for those turning 70½ after December 31, 2019. It is not the correct RMD age for those turning 72 after December 31, 2022.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - The SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022 increased the required minimum distribution (RMD) age to 73 for individuals who turn 72 after December 31, 2022. This means that the first RMD must be taken by April 1 of the year following the year they turn 73.
whyWrong.3: Why option C is WRONG - The age of 75 will be the RMD age for those born after 1960, beginning in 2033. It is not the correct RMD age for those turning 72 after December 31, 2022.

questions.educational: The required minimum distribution (RMD) rules are designed to ensure that retirement savings are eventually distributed and taxed. The SECURE Act and SECURE 2.0 Act have modified the RMD age, reflecting increasing life expectancies. Understanding these changes is crucial for tax planning and advising clients on their retirement distributions, a key area covered in the Individual Taxation section of the EA exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates and ages specified in retirement distribution questions, as these details determine which RMD rules apply. Remember that the SECURE 2.0 Act made significant changes to RMD rules.
questions.memoryAid: Think '73 for '23' – turning 72 after 2022, RMD at 73.
questions.authorityRef: IRC §401(a)(9)(C); SECURE Act of 2019; SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022
questions.commonMistake: The exam exploits the misconception that the RMD age has remained constant. Many candidates incorrectly remember the previous RMD ages of 70½ or 72, failing to account for the changes introduced by the SECURE Act and SECURE 2.0 Act. It's crucial to know the current applicable age based on the individual's birthdate.

questions.question: Tom failed to take his full RMD from his traditional IRA for the 2023 tax year. Under SECURE 2.0, the excise tax for failure to take an RMD is:
options: 10% of shortfall, a rate applicable prior to 2023
50% of shortfall, unless a waiver is successfully filed
25% of shortfall (10% if corrected promptly)
100% of shortfall, if the IRS deems the error egregious
questions.explanation: SECURE 2.0 reduced the RMD penalty from 50% to 25% of the shortfall. Further reduced to 10% if corrected in a timely manner during the correction window.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The excise tax is 25% of the shortfall under SECURE 2.0, not 10%. The 10% rate only applies if corrected promptly.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The excise tax is 25% of the shortfall under SECURE 2.0, not 50%. While waivers exist, the base penalty is still incorrect.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - SECURE 2.0 reduced the excise tax for failing to take a required minimum distribution (RMD) from 50% to 25% of the shortfall. If the failure is corrected within a timely manner during the correction window, the excise tax is further reduced to 10% of the shortfall.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The excise tax is 25% of the shortfall under SECURE 2.0, not 100%. Even in cases of severe error, the penalty doesn't reach 100%.

questions.educational: The required minimum distribution (RMD) rules are designed to ensure that retirement savings are eventually distributed and taxed. SECURE 2.0's reduction in the excise tax penalty reflects a policy shift towards encouraging compliance rather than imposing harsh penalties, and recognizes that errors can occur. Understanding RMD rules and penalties is crucial for tax planning and advising clients on retirement distributions, a key area for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to dates and legislative changes, especially those introduced by SECURE 2.0, as they often impact retirement planning questions. Remember the reduced RMD penalty and the conditions for the further reduction to 10%.
questions.memoryAid: RMD Penalty: 25% (Standard), 10% (Corrected Promptly). Think 'Quarter' and 'Dime' for the penalty amounts.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the changes to the excise tax for failure to take RMDs under SECURE 2.0. The exam exploits the confusion between the old 50% penalty and the new reduced rates. Test-takers often incorrectly assume the penalty remains at 50% or that it's a flat 10%.

questions.question: Lisa contributes to her employer's 401(k) plan. She is considering contributing to the Roth 401(k) option. Roth 401(k) contributions:
options: Are deductible from your gross income in the year made.
Are limited to $7,000 annually, regardless of age or catch-up provisions.
Are after-tax, with qualified distributions tax-free
Reduce employment taxes for both the employee and employer.
questions.explanation: Roth 401(k) contributions are after-tax (not deductible) but qualified distributions are completely tax-free. The contribution limit matches traditional 401(k) ($23,000 for 2024).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Roth 401(k) contributions are never deductible; they are made with after-tax dollars.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The $7,000 limit is incorrect and doesn't account for potential catch-up contributions for those age 50 and over. The actual limit changes annually.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Roth 401(k) contributions are made with after-tax dollars, meaning you pay income tax on the money before contributing it. The significant benefit is that qualified distributions in retirement, including both contributions and earnings, are entirely tax-free, provided certain conditions are met (e.g., being at least age 59 1/2 and the account being open for at least five years).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Roth 401(k) contributions do not reduce employment taxes. Only pre-tax contributions to traditional 401(k) plans reduce taxable income, and therefore, employment taxes.

questions.educational: Roth 401(k)s are powerful retirement savings tools that offer tax-free growth and withdrawals in retirement. This contrasts with traditional 401(k)s, which offer a tax deduction now but are taxed upon withdrawal. The choice between Roth and traditional depends on your current and expected future tax bracket. Understanding these differences is crucial for advising clients on optimal retirement planning strategies, a core competency tested on the EA exam.
questions.examTip: Remember to consider the client's current and projected future tax bracket when advising on Roth vs. traditional retirement accounts. A lower current tax bracket often favors Roth contributions, while a higher current tax bracket may favor traditional contributions.
questions.memoryAid: Roth: 'Retire On Tax-free Harvest'
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse Roth 401(k) contributions with traditional 401(k) contributions. A common trap is assuming that Roth contributions are deductible, similar to traditional 401(k) contributions. The exam also tests knowledge of the after-tax nature of Roth contributions and the tax-free treatment of qualified distributions.

questions.question: Mark has a Roth 401(k) account through his employer. He is approaching retirement and wants to understand the RMD rules. Under SECURE 2.0, Roth 401(k) accounts are:
options: Subject to RMDs during owner's lifetime
Taxable upon distribution
Exempt from RMDs starting in 2024
Subject to 10-year rule
questions.explanation: SECURE 2.0 eliminated lifetime RMDs for Roth 401(k) accounts starting in 2024, matching Roth IRA rules.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Prior to SECURE 2.0, Roth 401(k) accounts were subject to Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) during the owner's lifetime. However, SECURE 2.0 changed this, making this option incorrect starting in 2024.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Qualified distributions from a Roth 401(k) are tax-free, assuming certain conditions are met, such as being held for at least five years and meeting an age requirement (typically 59 1/2). This option incorrectly states that distributions are taxable.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - SECURE 2.0 Act eliminated the requirement for Roth 401(k) account holders to take Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) during their lifetime, effective January 1, 2024. This aligns the treatment of Roth 401(k)s with Roth IRAs, which have always been exempt from lifetime RMDs.
whyWrong.3: Why option C is WRONG - The 10-year rule generally applies to designated beneficiaries of retirement accounts who inherit after the original account owner's death. While this rule can impact Roth 401(k) accounts after death, it doesn't directly relate to the lifetime RMD requirements, which is the focus of the question.

questions.educational: The elimination of lifetime RMDs for Roth 401(k) accounts under SECURE 2.0 simplifies retirement planning and enhances the appeal of Roth accounts. This change encourages individuals to save more in Roth accounts, as they can defer distributions indefinitely during their lifetime, allowing for continued tax-free growth. Understanding the nuances of retirement account rules, including RMDs and Roth conversions, is crucial for Enrolled Agents advising clients on tax-efficient retirement strategies.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the effective dates of new tax laws like SECURE 2.0, as they often have a delayed implementation that can affect your answer.
questions.memoryAid: Roth 401(k)s now 'Retire' from RMDs!
questions.commonMistake: Many test-takers are unaware of the SECURE 2.0 Act provision that eliminates RMDs for Roth 401(k) accounts. The exam exploits the misconception that Roth 401(k) accounts are always subject to RMDs during the owner's lifetime, similar to traditional retirement accounts. Candidates often overlook the recent legislative change.

questions.question: During divorce proceedings, the court orders a division of Mark's 401(k) at Vanguard. A Qualified Domestic Relations Order (QDRO) is issued. What does a QDRO allow in this situation?
options: Tax-free conversions of the entire 401(k) balance into a Roth IRA, regardless of income limits or tax implications for the recipient spouse.
Loans from retirement accounts to either spouse, allowing them to borrow up to 50% of the vested balance to cover legal fees or other divorce-related expenses.
Division of retirement plan assets incident to divorce without triggering income or penalty
Early withdrawal without penalty from any retirement account, including IRAs and other qualified plans, for either spouse involved in the divorce proceedings.
questions.explanation: A QDRO divides employer retirement plan assets in divorce. The alternate payee (spouse) can receive without penalty; it becomes taxable when distributed to recipient.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A QDRO facilitates division, not conversion. While conversions are possible, they aren't the primary function of a QDRO and are subject to specific rules and potential tax consequences for the recipient.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A QDRO does not authorize loans. While loans are sometimes permitted from 401(k)s, they are unrelated to the QDRO process and are subject to the plan's loan provisions and potential tax implications.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - A Qualified Domestic Relations Order (QDRO) is a court order that allows for the division of retirement plan assets between spouses during a divorce without triggering immediate income tax consequences or the 10% early withdrawal penalty under IRC Section 72(t). The assets are transferred to the alternate payee (ex-spouse) and taxed when distributed to them.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A QDRO only allows penalty-free withdrawals for the alternate payee (ex-spouse) from the *divided* assets, not from *any* retirement account. It doesn't grant a blanket penalty waiver for all accounts.

questions.educational: A QDRO is crucial in divorce settlements involving retirement assets. Without a QDRO, a transfer of retirement funds to a spouse would be considered a taxable distribution to the plan participant. The QDRO ensures that the transfer is treated as a division of property, not a taxable event, aligning with the principles of equitable distribution in divorce law and retirement plan regulations.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that a QDRO only applies to *qualified* retirement plans (like 401(k)s and pensions), not IRAs. IRAs can be transferred incident to divorce without a QDRO.
questions.memoryAid: QDRO = Qualified Divorce Retirement Order
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates misunderstand the purpose and effect of a QDRO. A common trap is assuming that a QDRO allows for actions beyond the division of retirement assets incident to divorce. The exam tests whether you understand that a QDRO facilitates a tax-free transfer of assets without triggering penalties.

questions.question: As part of their divorce settlement, Sarah is awarded John's traditional IRA. The IRA is transferred directly to Sarah. Regarding this transfer, which of the following is true?
options: It must be rolled over into Sarah's own IRA within 60 days to avoid taxation.
It's subject to a 10% penalty if Sarah is under age 59 1/2 at the time of the transfer.
It's tax-free and becomes the recipient spouse's IRA
It's a taxable distribution to John, reportable in the year the transfer occurs, as he no longer owns the assets.
questions.explanation: IRA transfers incident to divorce are tax-free. The receiving spouse treats it as their own IRA, not an inherited IRA. No QDRO needed for IRAs.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A transfer incident to divorce is not a rollover, and is not subject to the 60-day rule. It's a direct transfer.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Transfers incident to divorce are not treated as distributions, so the 10% penalty does not apply, regardless of Sarah's age.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Under IRC Section 408(d)(6), a transfer of an IRA from one spouse to another incident to a divorce is treated as a non-taxable event. The IRA becomes the property of the transferee spouse, who then owns and controls it as their own IRA, not as an inherited IRA.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Transfers incident to divorce are not taxable events. The transfer is considered a change of ownership, not a distribution to the original owner.

questions.educational: The rule allowing tax-free transfers of retirement assets incident to divorce is designed to facilitate equitable property settlements without creating an undue tax burden. This aligns with the broader goal of divorce settlements, which aim to divide marital assets fairly. Understanding the nuances of retirement asset transfers is crucial for Enrolled Agents advising clients going through a divorce, as it directly impacts their tax planning and financial well-being.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that qualified domestic relations orders (QDROs) are generally required for dividing qualified plans (like 401(k)s), but not for IRAs. IRA transfers incident to divorce are handled directly without a QDRO.
questions.memoryAid: Divorce IRA = Direct, Don't Tax!
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse transfers incident to divorce with regular distributions or rollovers. A common trap is assuming that a transfer pursuant to a divorce decree is a taxable event or requires a rollover. The exam tests whether you understand the specific tax treatment of such transfers.

questions.question: Maria contributed $2,000 to her 401(k) this year. She wants to know if she qualifies for the Saver's Credit. The availability and rate of the Saver's Credit depends on:
options: Age of taxpayer and number of dependents claimed, with a minimum age of 21
Years of service with the employer sponsoring the 401(k) plan, impacting vesting schedules
AGI and filing status, with maximum credit rate of 50%
Type of retirement account and associated tax penalties for early withdrawal before age 59 1/2
questions.explanation: Saver's Credit rates are 50%, 20%, or 10% based on AGI and filing status. It phases out completely at moderate income levels (e.g., $76,500 MFJ for 2024).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While there is a minimum age of 18 to be eligible, the *amount* of the Saver's Credit is not directly impacted by the taxpayer's age or number of dependents. The credit is based on AGI and filing status.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Years of service affect vesting in employer contributions, but have no bearing on the Saver's Credit, which is based on the taxpayer's contribution and AGI.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - The Saver's Credit is designed to encourage low-to-moderate income taxpayers to save for retirement. The credit rate (50%, 20%, or 10%) is determined by the taxpayer's Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) and filing status, with the 50% rate being the maximum. The credit phases out entirely at higher income levels.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The *type* of retirement account (401k, IRA, etc.) only determines *eligibility* for the Saver's Credit, not the credit rate or amount. The amount is based on AGI and filing status.

questions.educational: The Saver's Credit is a nonrefundable credit intended to incentivize retirement savings for individuals with modest incomes. By offering a tax credit based on AGI and filing status, the government aims to encourage saving behavior that might otherwise be difficult for lower-income individuals. Understanding the AGI thresholds for each filing status is crucial, as the credit phases out completely at higher income levels, making it a targeted benefit.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the AGI thresholds for the Saver's Credit based on filing status, as these values are updated annually and are frequently tested. Remember that the credit is nonrefundable, meaning it can only reduce your tax liability to zero.
questions.memoryAid: SAG: Saver's credit depends on Status and AGI.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates focus on the contribution amount and forget the AGI limitations and filing status requirements for the Saver's Credit. The exam exploits the misconception that any contribution to a retirement account automatically qualifies for the credit. Test-takers often overlook the income-based eligibility criteria.

questions.question: Green Acres Farm is struggling financially and negotiates with its bank to have $50,000 of debt forgiven. Immediately before the debt discharge, Green Acres' liabilities exceeded its assets. COD income is excluded in insolvency to the extent:
options: COD is always taxable unless specifically excluded by the IRS code.
COD is excluded only in bankruptcy proceedings under Title 11 of the U.S. Code.
The taxpayer is insolvent immediately before the discharge
There is full exclusion for any insolvency, regardless of the amount of debt discharged.
questions.explanation: Insolvency exclusion applies to COD income to the extent of insolvency (liabilities exceeding FMV of assets) immediately before discharge. Tax attributes must be reduced.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - COD is not always taxable; insolvency and bankruptcy are exceptions. This option fails to acknowledge these exceptions.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While bankruptcy is an exclusion, it is not the *only* exclusion. Insolvency also allows for exclusion of COD income.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - The insolvency exclusion for COD income is determined by the extent to which the taxpayer is insolvent immediately before the debt discharge. This is the key factor in determining the excludable amount, as per IRC Section 108.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The exclusion for insolvency is limited to the amount of insolvency. This option incorrectly states that there is full exclusion regardless of the amount.

questions.educational: The insolvency exclusion for COD income aims to provide relief to taxpayers who are already in a financially distressed situation. By allowing them to exclude COD income up to the amount of their insolvency, it prevents them from being taxed on income they effectively didn't receive, as their liabilities already exceed their assets. This exclusion is a key component of understanding income inclusions and exclusions, a core area tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember to calculate the amount of insolvency immediately BEFORE the debt discharge, and that this amount is the maximum COD income that can be excluded. Be prepared to calculate the amount of insolvency on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: I Before D: Insolvency is measured Immediately Before Discharge.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates forget the specific requirements for excluding COD income due to insolvency. A common trap is assuming that insolvency always leads to full exclusion, regardless of the extent of insolvency. The exam tests whether you understand that the exclusion is limited to the amount of insolvency immediately before the discharge.

questions.question: Due to insolvency, Johnson Enterprises excluded $75,000 of COD income from its taxable income. When COD income is excluded, tax attributes must be reduced in what order?
options: No reduction required unless the taxpayer elects to reduce the basis of depreciable property before any other tax attributes are considered.
General business credit, minimum tax credit, capital loss carryover, then basis in assets, followed by any net operating loss carryforward.
NOL, general business credit, capital loss carryover, basis, passive activity loss, foreign tax credit carryover
Basis first, specifically reducing the basis of real property held for investment, then personal property, and finally inventory if necessary.
questions.explanation: Excluded COD income requires attribute reduction in order: NOL, general business credit carryover, minimum tax credit, capital loss carryover, basis, passive activity loss, foreign tax credit carryover.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Tax attribute reduction is mandatory when COD income is excluded due to insolvency or bankruptcy, regardless of any election to reduce the basis of depreciable property first.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Credits are generally reduced after losses and basis. The order presented is also incorrect for the credit reductions themselves.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - When COD income is excluded from gross income due to insolvency or bankruptcy, the taxpayer must reduce certain tax attributes in a specific order. The correct order is: Net Operating Loss (NOL), general business credit carryover, capital loss carryover, basis, passive activity loss carryover, and foreign tax credit carryover. This prevents a double benefit from both excluding the income and retaining the full value of these tax benefits.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Basis is not the first attribute reduced. Net operating losses are reduced before basis, and the specific order of asset basis reduction is also incorrect.

questions.educational: The exclusion of COD income is a tax break granted to taxpayers in financial distress. To prevent a double benefit, the tax law requires a reduction of certain tax attributes. This reduction ensures that the taxpayer does not permanently escape taxation on the forgiven debt, as the reduced attributes will result in higher taxable income or lower deductions in future years. Understanding the order of attribute reduction is crucial for accurate tax planning and compliance.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific facts of the question regarding COD income. Determine if the exclusion applies (insolvency or bankruptcy) and then apply the correct order of attribute reduction. Remember that the order matters!
questions.memoryAid: NOL-GBC-CLC-Basis-PAL-FTC: "Never Go Back, Carry Loss Carefully, Basis Provides Always Lasting, Forever Taxing Credits" (NOL, General Business Credit, Capital Loss Carryover, Basis, Passive Activity Loss, Foreign Tax Credit Carryover)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the order of tax attribute reduction. The exam exploits the misconception that basis is reduced first, while NOL is actually reduced before basis. The correct order is: 1) NOL, 2) General Business Credit, 3) Capital Loss Carryover, 4) Basis, 5) Passive Activity Loss Carryover, 6) Foreign Tax Credit Carryover. Option D is tempting because basis is a common tax attribute, but it's not the first to be reduced.

questions.question: Due to a discharge of indebtedness, Lisa reduced certain tax attributes. Which form is used to report this reduction?
options: Reporting foreign income and associated tax credits on Form 1116
Calculating and reporting self-employment tax using Schedule SE (Form 1040)
Reduction of tax attributes due to discharge of indebtedness
Deducting passive activity losses carried over from prior years on Form 8582
questions.explanation: Form 982 reports excluded COD income and the required reduction of tax attributes under IRC §108.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 1116 is used for claiming the foreign tax credit, not for reporting the reduction of tax attributes due to discharge of indebtedness.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Schedule SE is used to calculate self-employment tax, not to report the reduction of tax attributes due to discharge of indebtedness.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Form 982, Reduction of Tax Attributes Due to Discharge of Indebtedness (and Section 1082 Basis Adjustment), is used to report the exclusion of income from the discharge of indebtedness (COD income) under IRC §108 and the corresponding reduction of tax attributes, such as net operating losses, credits, and basis of assets. This form details how the taxpayer is electing to treat the excluded COD income and how their tax attributes are being reduced.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 8582 is used to calculate the allowable passive activity loss deduction, not to report the reduction of tax attributes due to discharge of indebtedness.

questions.educational: The discharge of indebtedness (COD) generally results in taxable income. However, IRC §108 provides exceptions allowing taxpayers in certain situations (e.g., bankruptcy, insolvency) to exclude COD income from gross income. In exchange for this exclusion, the taxpayer must reduce certain tax attributes to prevent a double benefit. Understanding COD income and the related tax attribute reduction is crucial for the EA exam, particularly when advising clients facing financial difficulties.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that Form 982 is directly linked to IRC §108 and the concept of excluding COD income while reducing tax attributes. If a question mentions COD income and insolvency, consider Form 982 as a potential answer.
questions.memoryAid: 982: 'Nine-eighty-two, debt relief for you!' (Helps remember it's about debt discharge).
questions.commonMistake: Many test-takers incorrectly assume that attribute reduction is reported on a general tax form. The exam exploits the misconception that there isn't a specific form for reporting reduction of tax attributes due to discharge of indebtedness. Options A, B, and D are all real forms, but they address different tax situations.

questions.question: After battling cancer for several years, David passed away. As a result, his outstanding student loan balance of $30,000 was forgiven. Under TCJA, this student loan forgiveness is:
options: Always taxable as ordinary income
Subject to 10% penalty if under 59 1/2
Excluded from income through 2025
Deductible as an itemized deduction
questions.explanation: Student loan discharge due to death or total and permanent disability is excluded from income through 2025 under TCJA provision. PSLF and qualifying IDR may also be excluded.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While some debt forgiveness is taxable, student loan forgiveness due to death is generally excluded from income, especially through 2025 under TCJA provisions.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The 10% penalty generally applies to early distributions from retirement accounts, not student loan forgiveness. Student loan forgiveness is an income issue, not a distribution issue.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) included a provision that excludes from gross income student loan discharges due to death or total and permanent disability. This exclusion is currently set to expire after 2025, meaning that without further legislative action, such discharges could become taxable income again starting in 2026.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While some student loan interest may be deductible, the forgiven amount itself is not deductible. Deductions reduce taxable income; exclusions prevent it from being taxed in the first place.

questions.educational: The general rule is that cancellation of debt (COD) is considered taxable income because the taxpayer received a benefit (the loan proceeds) but did not fully repay it. However, Congress can create exceptions to this rule for policy reasons. The TCJA's exclusion for student loan discharges due to death or disability aims to provide relief to individuals and their families during difficult times, aligning with the broader goal of tax policy to address social and economic concerns.
questions.examTip: Remember that the TCJA provisions, including the student loan discharge exclusion, are subject to change. On the EA exam, pay close attention to the tax year specified in the question to determine if a particular provision is still in effect.
questions.memoryAid: TCJA = Temporary Changes Just Ask!
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the exception for student loan forgiveness due to death or disability. The exam exploits the misconception that all canceled debt is taxable income. While generally cancellation of debt is taxable, there are exceptions, including student loan forgiveness in specific circumstances.

questions.question: TechForward Inc. offers its employees a student loan repayment assistance program. Regarding employer student loan repayment assistance (Section 127), which of the following is true?
options: Not permitted under current IRS guidelines for 501(c)(3) organizations.
Always taxable income to the employee, even if structured as a fringe benefit.
Excludable up to $5,250 annually through 2025
Unlimited exclusion if the employee works in a designated STEM field.
questions.explanation: Through 2025, employers can pay up to $5,250 of employee student loans tax-free under the educational assistance program rules (§127).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Employer student loan repayment assistance is permitted under Section 127 of the IRS code. It is not limited to non-profits.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While generally considered taxable, Section 127 allows for an exclusion up to $5,250 annually.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - Section 127 of the Internal Revenue Code allows employers to provide up to $5,250 annually in tax-free educational assistance, which can include student loan repayment. This provision was extended through 2025, making this the correct answer.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The exclusion is capped at $5,250 annually, regardless of the employee's field of work.

questions.educational: Section 127 was created to encourage employers to invest in their employees' education and skills development, including helping them manage student loan debt. This provision aligns with broader tax policy goals of promoting workforce development and economic growth. Understanding educational assistance programs is crucial for Enrolled Agents, as it impacts both employee income tax liability and employer payroll tax obligations.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates associated with temporary tax provisions like the Section 127 student loan repayment assistance exclusion, as these are frequently tested and subject to change.
questions.memoryAid: 127 - Five Two Fifty (5250) - Remember the section number and the dollar amount.
questions.commonMistake: Many test-takers are unaware of the employer student loan repayment assistance program exclusion. The exam exploits the misconception that employer-provided benefits are always taxable to the employee. While some benefits are taxable, Section 127 provides a specific exclusion for student loan repayment assistance, up to a limit.

questions.question: Maria and David are planning for their children's future education. They established a 529 plan several years ago. Which of the following expenses are considered qualified expenses for a 529 plan? A) Transportation expenses | B) Room and board only | C) Tuition, fees, books, supplies, equipment, room and board (if enrolled half-time), and K-12 tuition up to $10,000 | D) Tuition only
options: Transportation expenses, including commuting costs to and from the educational institution, are qualified 529 expenses.
Room and board expenses are qualified only if the beneficiary is enrolled at least full-time and living in university-owned housing.
Tuition, fees, books, supplies, equipment, room and board (if enrolled half-time), and K-12 tuition up to $10,000
Tuition for courses directly related to the beneficiary's degree program is the only qualified expense for a 529 plan.
questions.explanation: 529 qualified expenses include higher education tuition, fees, books, supplies, equipment, room and board (at least half-time), computers, and K-12 tuition up to $10,000/year.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Transportation expenses are generally not qualified 529 expenses, with very limited exceptions for special needs students.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Room and board can be qualified even if the student isn't living in university housing and if they are enrolled at least half-time, not full-time.
whyWrong.2: Why option D is CORRECT - This option accurately reflects the qualified expenses for a 529 plan. These include tuition, fees, books, supplies, equipment, room and board (if the student is enrolled at least half-time), and K-12 tuition up to $10,000 per year per beneficiary, as outlined in IRS Publication 970.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Qualified expenses extend beyond tuition to include fees, books, supplies, and equipment, as well as room and board under certain conditions, and K-12 tuition up to $10,000.

questions.educational: Section 529 plans are designed to encourage saving for future education costs. The tax advantages, such as tax-free growth and tax-free withdrawals for qualified expenses, are a significant incentive. Understanding what constitutes a qualified expense is crucial for clients to maximize these benefits and avoid potential penalties on non-qualified withdrawals. This topic directly relates to advising clients on tax-advantaged savings strategies, a core competency for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the details regarding enrollment status (at least half-time for room and board) and the K-12 tuition limit ($10,000) when determining qualified 529 plan expenses. Misinterpreting these details is a common error.
questions.memoryAid: T-F-B-S-E-R-K: Think of 'Tuition, Fees, Books, Supplies, Equipment, Room & Board, K-12' to remember the main categories of qualified 529 expenses.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates have an incomplete understanding of qualified 529 plan expenses. The exam exploits the misconception that 529 plans only cover tuition. While tuition is a major component, qualified expenses also include fees, books, supplies, equipment, and, under certain conditions, room and board and K-12 tuition.

questions.question: John inherited a 529 plan from his grandmother. Under SECURE 2.0, he's considering rolling the 529 into a Roth IRA for himself. This 529 to Roth IRA rollover is permitted if: A) Always permitted without limits | B) Only if 529 earnings exceed basis | C) Account was open 15+ years, lifetime $35,000 limit, subject to annual Roth limits | D) Beneficiary is over 59½
options: Always permitted without limits, provided the original beneficiary is maintained and contributions were made at least five years prior.
Only if 529 earnings exceed the original contribution basis, and the rollover is completed within the same calendar year the funds are distributed.
Account was open 15+ years, lifetime $35,000 limit, subject to annual Roth limits
Beneficiary is over 59½ and can demonstrate financial hardship, allowing a one-time rollover up to the current annual Roth IRA contribution limit.
